text:
KuaiRec (short-video recommendation). KuaiRec is a fully observed short-video recommendation dataset with dense
user–item interactions and rich user/video metadata. Since the raw data is near-dense, we induce sparsity to better match
realistic implicit-feedback regimes by uniformly subsampling 10% of interactions per user. We then construct user-level
splits with an 80%/10%/10% train/validation/test partition. For each split, we build sequential ranking instances by
conditioning on a user's most recent H=10 historical items and asking the model to rank a candidate set of C=30 items;
the candidate construction procedure is described in Appendix D. MovieLens (rating-based recommendation). MovieLens is a classic benchmark for rating-based recommendation that
captures long-term user preferences over movies. We use the same dataset splitting and processing protocol as used in
KuaiRec. ESCI (product search). ESCI represents product search, where the goal is to retrieve items most relevant to a textual
query rather than a user's interaction sequence. We follow the dataset's publicly released train–test split for training and
evaluation.3
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
For KuaiRec, following prior work (Gao et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2023b; Chen et al., 2024), we use the watch ratio—defined
as the ratio between the total watch time and the video length—as a proxy for user interest. Watch ratio provides a continuous,
fine-grained signal that reflects both user engagement and early abandonment behavior, and is commonly adopted as an
implicit relevance measure in short-video recommendation settings. We directly use the watch ratio value as the relevance
score when evaluating item utility. For ML-1M, explicit user feedback is available in the form of discrete ratings ranging from 1 to 5. We treat higher ratings as
stronger expressions of user interest and transform ratings into relevance scores via the exponential mapping 2rating −1. We use the following instruction across datasets:
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
Need: Maximizing Interest Rank them to maximize the user's expected watch ratio (likelihood of long/complete viewing) based on their history—because higher watch ratio signals stronger engagement and improves retention. Also keep in mind to recommend
items that strongly align with the user's known interests. Niche discovery aims to recommend items that expose users to previously unseen topical items while remaining relevant to
their interests. In KuaiRec, we implement niche discovery using video topic tags. For a given user history, we identify the set of topics
the user has previously interacted with, and label a candidate video as niche if its topic does not appear in this history. Candidates without valid topic annotations are treated as non-niche. To construct a ground-truth ranking that balances relevance and novelty, we start from a base relevance score obtained via
embedding-based retrieval. We then apply a multiplicative niche bonus to candidates labeled as niche,
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
scoreniche(v) = scorebase(v) · (1 + α), where α > 0 controls the strength of the novelty preference. The resulting scores induce a ranking that prioritizes novel-topic
items while preserving relevance among both niche and non-niche candidates. This formulation explicitly counteracts
exposure bias by amplifying under-explored items without discarding similarity-based relevance. 3https://github.com/amazon-science/esci-data FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning For ML-1M, niche discovery is defined analogously by identifying movies whose genres have not appeared in the user's
interaction history.
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
We use the following instruction across datasets: Need: Niche Discovery Keep in mind to recommend not only items aligned with the user's interests but also items that are niche (topics the
user hasn't interacted with), but that are plausibly relevant and could enrich their experience. Trending recommendation aims to surface items that are both relevant to the user and currently popular across the platform,
capturing short-term collective interest and temporal dynamics. In KuaiRec, we construct trending signals using recent interaction frequency. For each candidate video, we compute its
trend score as the number of user interactions occurring within a sliding one-day window prior to the query time. To construct a ground-truth ranking that balances personalization and global popularity, we combine semantic relevance and
temporal trend signals. Candidate videos are first retrieved using embedding-based similarity to the user's recent interaction
history. Both the similarity scores and the one-day trend counts are independently normalized via min–max normalization. The final relevance score is computed as the combination, scoretrend(v) = α · simnorm(v) + (1 −α) · trendnorm(v), (4) where α ∈(0, 1) controls the trade-off between personalized relevance and global trending popularity (we use α = 0.7
across experiments). The resulting scores induce a ranking that prioritizes items that are both timely and aligned with the
user's interests. For ML-1M, trending recommendation is defined analogously using recent 1-day activity. We use the following instruction across datasets:
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
Need: Trending Promotion Keep in mind to recommend not only items aligned with the user's interests but also items that are popular in the last
24 hours (provided as the number of watches in the last day), as long as they are relevant to the user. Amazon's official ESCI dataset annotates each (query, item) pair into four relevance categories—Exact (E), satisfying all
query constraints; Substitute (S), partially matching but usable as a replacement; Complement (C), not matching the query
itself but useful alongside an exact item; and Irrelevant (I). We follow these categories and the official graded scoring
scheme, mapping them to {1.0, 0.1, 0.01, 0.0} for {E, S, C, I}, respectively.
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
We use the following instruction for product search: Your task is to rerank these candidate products by how well they match the user's search query. Consider how closely
each product's title, description, and attributes align with the query intent. Give higher ranks to products that are
exact or highly relevant matches (e.g., correct type, brand, or specifications). Lower the rank of products that are
complementary or unrelated to the query.
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
To ensure scalable training and evaluation, and to facilitate future extensions across datasets and tasks, we subsample each
training set to 5,000 instances and each test set to 1,000 instances for all datasets and recommendation settings. FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning We perform reinforcement learning (RL) post-training on 4× NVIDIA A100 GPUs using the Verl 4 library. Unless otherwise
specified, all experiments use full-parameter training with learning rate 1 × 10−6.
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
To stabilize optimization and constrain policy drift, we include a KL regularization term against a reference policy with KL
loss coefficient 0.01. We additionally apply entropy regularization to encourage exploration and avoid premature collapse,
using entropy coefficient 0.005. For rollout generation, we use vLLM as the inference backend to efficiently sample sequences during RL training, improving
throughput and reducing end-to-end training latency. We construct the candidate set using a fixed semantic retriever following STAR (Lee et al., 2025), a state-of-the-art trainingfree retrieval framework based on large language model embeddings. Each item is first converted into a natural-language
description using its available metadata, and embedded using the text-embedding-004 model from the Gemini
embedding family (Lee et al., 2024). Given a user interaction history of length H = 10, candidate items are retrieved by cosine similarity in the embedding space. To account for temporal preference dynamics, we form a query embedding as a temporally discounted average of historical
item embeddings, optionally weighted by interaction strength (e.g., ratings or watch ratios), following the original STAR
formulation. The candidate set consists of the top-K most similar items, excluding those already interacted with by the user. The retriever is kept frozen throughout training and evaluation.
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
This setup reflects realistic recommendation pipelines, where
candidate generation is typically performed by a pre-trained, independently maintained retrieval system, while downstream
ranking models are optimized on a fixed candidate pool. In this section we detail the relevant information we give to the models. We give the same set of metadata for embedding
models in retrieval as well as for LLMs in reranking. For short videos, we leverage rich categorical and item annotations provided by the dataset. Each video
is converted into a textual description composed of its caption, hierarchical category path, and optional tag information: Video caption: <caption>
Category path: <level 1> > <level 2> > <level 3>
Video tag: <tag> Movies (MovieLens-1M). For movies, we construct a concise textual description consisting of the movie title, release
year, and genre information: Movie Title: <title>
Movie Year: <year>
Movie genres: <genre 1>, <genre 2>, ...

Addressing Invalid Output Prior work (Ren & Sutherland, 2025; Deng et al., 2025) shows that negative advantage can
induce invalid outputs by "squeezing" the policy out of valid regimes. We observe a similar trend: as models improve
ranking quality, they may also emit invalid item IDs. We view this as expected behavior: the model should avoid low-quality
items. Thus, we treat invalid IDs as low-confidence predictions. For fair evaluation, when an output contains fewer than K
valid items, we randomly pad the list with the rest candidate items to reach length K. This padding has negligible impact on
our metrics, since they are dominated by the top-ranked items.

text:
Task Definition and Challenges Given a user's interaction context which consists of historical items and observed
engagement signals, we aim to predict the user's potential interaction with a candidate item. This prediction task is inherently
challenging, subsuming the reranking problem considered in this work. Accordingly, the goal of the critic is not to produce
perfectly accurate engagement predictions, but rather to extract and provide useful learning signals that can be exploited
during the reinforcement learning stage. In recommendation settings, such engagement signals (e.g., watch ratio or dwell time) exhibit two properties. First, they are
long-tailed, with a small fraction of interactions accounting for a disproportionate share of total engagement. Second, they
are heteroscedastic: the intrinsic noise of user feedback varies substantially across contexts, users, and items. As a result,
both the expected engagement level and the uncertainty of this estimate depend on the input context.
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
Critic Architecture We employ a two-tower MLP-based critic (see 5 for details) that encodes user history and candidate
items into a shared embedding space, followed by a lightweight interaction network. The critic outputs both a predicted
mean engagement ˆµ(X) and an input-dependent variance σ2(X). The architecture is intentionally simple, as the critic
serves as a training signal rather than a primary ranking model.
title: FlexRec: Adapting LLM-based Recommenders for Flexible Needs via Reinforcement Learning

text:
Variance-Weighted Gaussian Likelihood To model heteroscedastic engagement noise, we use recent advances in
heteroscedastic regression using neural networks (Seitzer et al., 2022). Let Y denote the observed engagement signal and X
the input context. The critic predicts a mean ˆµ(X) and variance σ2(X). We minimize the following loss: 1 (Y −ˆµ(X))2
Lβ-NLL = E(X,Y ) σ2(X) log σ2(X) + . (5)
2 2σ2(X) We follow the original work and use β = 1 Training On both KuaiRec and ML-1M, we first conduct a 8:1:1 split on all interactions data. Then we train the critic for
30 epochs and pick the checkpoint with the best MSE. Results Table 4 reports the critic's performance. While the predicted values are positively correlated with the ground-truth
scores—suggesting the critic can provide useful reward signals for RLVR—they still exhibit substantial variance and
nontrivial prediction error. This motivates explicitly modeling uncertainty during RL to downweight unreliable signals and
avoid destabilizing updates.

FLEXREC can serve as a universal, general-purpose recommender capable of handling heterogeneous objectives within
a single model. Finally, we note that the generated reasoning is valuable not only for improving prediction, but also for
offering explainability and transparency—properties that are often missing in traditional recommender systems (Ge et al.,
2024). Need: Maximizing Interest Prompt:
Given user history (in chronological order): . . . Rank them to maximize the user's expected watch ratio (likelihood of

retention. Also keep in mind to recommend items that strongly align with the user's known interests. Model Response:

Your task is to rerank these candidate products by how well they
match the user's search query. Consider how closely each product's title, description, and attributes align with the
query intent. Give higher ranks to products that are exact or highly relevant matches (e.g., correct type, brand, or
specifications). Lower the rank of products that are complementary or unrelated to the query.
